interactions.summary: A student asks why a new global economic system or consortium would not replace the U.S.-centric system after collapse, as earlier empires were replaced.
Jiang answers that the U.S. dollar is a contract system backed by military enforcement, and no other country or coalition wants to absorb the reserve-currency cost or fight everywhere to enforce it.
The student follows up by asking whether countries such as Japan, Germany, or America could jointly build a global economic system.

speaker_notes.note: These segments are audience/student questions despite diarization labels; surrounding answer structure makes attribution clear.

claims.claim: The U.S. dollar functions as a global contract system: trade promises are honored because a military enforcement mechanism stands behind the contract.
Reserve-currency status taxes and strains the issuing economy because it must absorb global liquidity, inflation, and financialization.
No nation besides America is willing to absorb both the economic cost of global reserve currency and the military burden of enforcing it anywhere in the world.
A cooperative replacement system among states is unsustainable because participants must negotiate who benefits, while empire can impose the answer by power.

signature_moments.moment: The dollar is a contract backed by someone who can beat you up if you break it.
signature_moments.source_phrase: someone has to come beat you up
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It gives a brutal, simple model of money as enforcement.

signature_moments.moment: No one will replace America because no one wants the cost of being America.
signature_moments.source_phrase: no one's willing to accept the cost
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It is the core answer to the student's replacement-empire question.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's model of the U.S. dollar order as a promise-and-enforcement regime backed by U.S. military power.

chronology_notes.note: This post-midterm exchange directly answers why Jiang expects fracture rather than a successor global economic order.
